draft.introduction: Book XIX of De Civitate Dei addresses the question of peace and the ultimate good. Augustine argues that the earthly city can never achieve true peace, which belongs only to the City of God. This book became foundational for Western political theology.

translation.text: The earthly city, which does not live by faith, seeks an earthly peace through the harmony of civic obedience and rule, the coordination of human wills regarding the things pertaining to mortal life. The heavenly city makes use of this peace in its pilgrimage.
translation.original_text: Terrena civitas, quae non vivit ex fide, terrenam pacem appetit, in eoque defigit imperandi oboediendique concordiam civium, ut sit eis de rebus ad mortalem vitam pertinentibus humanarum quaedam compositio voluntatum.

translator_notes.note: The distinction between earthly peace (pax terrena) and heavenly peace (pax caelestis) became the foundation of medieval political theology. Augustine’s argument that Christians “make use of” earthly peace during their “pilgrimage” provided a framework for Christian engagement with secular politics.
